pathophysiology_summary: Occlusion, friction and sweat from tight athletic wear during a long run damaged hair follicles and drove superficial (usually staphylococcal) infection/inflammation, producing hundreds of discrete 1–3 mm folliculocentric pustules with small erythematous halos and normal intervening skin — the hallmark of superficial folliculitis. There is no deep follicular nodule/abscess, confluent cellulitis or systemic involvement. Discriminators: hot-tub (pseudomonas) folliculitis follows spa/unchlorinated-water exposure in swimsuit-occlusion zones (absent here); acne shows comedones and a mixed chronic course, not a hyper-acute monomorphic frictional eruption; and the strictly superficial folliculocentric lesions without fever argue against systemic infection. Management is antibacterial washes, a topical antibacterial to the worst areas, itch relief and trigger control (loose breathable clothing, wash after exercise, avoid shaving the area), with a safety-net for progression to a furuncle/abscess or systemic infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Monomorphic folliculocentric pustules in friction areas after sweating.
Similar pustules but follows spa/unchlorinated-water exposure — screen for it.
Has comedones and a chronic course, not a hyper-acute monomorphic frictional eruption.
Screen for deep nodules/spreading redness/fever indicating furuncle/abscess or systemic spread.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise benign superficial folliculitis, exclude hot-tub exposure and deeper/systemic infection, and emphasise trigger control.

differential.evidence_basis: Superficial folliculocentric pustules, no hot-tub exposure, no deep/systemic features.
No deep nodules, spreading redness or fever — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: antibacterial wash + topical antibacterial + itch relief + trigger control; safety-net for boil/systemic features.

red_flags.description: Progression to a furuncle/abscess (large deep painful hot lump) or systemic infection (fever/chills, spreading deep redness)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Deeper follicular infection or systemic spread would need drainage/oral antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing systemic antibiotics/ED referral for benign superficial folliculitis.
Treating topically without addressing friction/occlusion/sweat, leading to recurrence.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Antibacterial wash + topical antibacterial + itch relief + trigger control; review/urgent care only if a furuncle/abscess or systemic features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-treating benign folliculitis, or missing progression to a deeper infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Superficial folliculitis: antiseptic/antibacterial washes and topical antibacterial for localised disease; address predisposing friction/occlusion; systemic antibiotics only if deep/extensive/systemic.
